text: The surgeon uses a left upper quadrant camera to visualize adhesions obstructing the placement of additional robotic ports. Two right-sided 5mm trocars are inserted, and the surgeon begins to dissect the adhesions to facilitate further trocar placement.
The surgeon works from right to left across the midline, navigating around the visible laparotomy scar.
The surgeon has placed robotic trocars, starting with two on the right side and one on the left side, in preparation for the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses the right-hand instrument to perform lysis of small bowel adhesions, initially hindered by the ileostomy. The omental adhesions are being carefully dissected to expose the deep pelvis.
The surgeon works on the left pelvic brim, revealing the colon beneath the omentum. The video shows a narrowed area of the proximal colon leading into the strictured anastomosis.

The surgeon continues to dissect and remove omental adhesions to expose the small bowel, which is adherent to the anastomosis. The omentum is carefully moved aside to facilitate further work on the small bowel.
The surgeon addresses the last adherent loop of bowel, which extends from the right pelvis to the anastomosis, carefully dissecting to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) in both ureters during the left-sided dissection, allowing for secure identification of the ureters without extensive dissection.
The surgeon continues to take down the loop of bowel, clearing it from the surgical field to begin mobilizing the descending sigmoid colon and upper rectum.
Imaging reveals a persistent posterior sinus tract extending from the anastomosis into the mesentery of the bowel leading to the anastomosis.
The surgeon initiates a lateral to medial dissection approach, carefully navigating over the ureter while maintaining the integrity of the plane above the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the mesentery of the descending colon, with the splenic flexure already mobilized from a previous operation.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels and enters the proper retroperitoneal plane. The ureter is visualized with green fluorescence from ICG, aiding in the dissection by providing clear identification of its location.

The surgeon continues the lateral to medial dissection, working down to the adherence of the faulty anastomosis on the left pelvic sidewall, ensuring the ureter remains distant from the surgical field.

The surgeon sweeps the gonadal vessels off the lateral aspect of the mesentery leading to the anastomosis. The suction device is held in the left hand, effectively clearing blood and smoke while providing retraction.

The surgeon encounters a challenging area near the ureter and switches techniques. The anastomosis is visible, and the rectum is identified. The pelvis appears closed off, prompting the surgeon to perform sharp dissection to open the area.
The surgeon isolates the rectum by entering the retroperitoneal plane, improving visibility for dissection from the inferior pelvis upwards. The ureter is visible in the background.

The surgeon mobilizes the left side of the rectal mesentery to separate the last part of the anastomosis from the pelvic sidewall, aiming to access the retroperitoneal plane for additional rectal length. The dissection appears to have minimal tension.
The surgeon enters the retrorectal space below the superior hemorrhoidal artery, following completion of lateral mobilization, to continue dissection in an area that had not been previously accessed.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery from medial to lateral, aiming to join the previously dissected space. The ureter is identified and preserved as the mesentery is lifted off the retroperitoneum towards the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to free the anastomosis, utilizing ICG mode to visualize the ureter and ensure the vessel sealer is safely away from it.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect through the mesentery to the specimen, navigating around a chronic sinus tract. The dissection appears to have traversed the proximal or distal part of the tract, with plans to address the proximal part as the surgeon moves underneath.
The surgeon clears thick tissue from the upper rectal mesentery, exposing the upper rectum.

The surgeon has taken the mesentery to the proximal and distal margins and is preparing for a hand-sewn end-to-end single-layer anastomosis. Monopolar cautery is used to transect the tissue, avoiding the use of staplers.
The surgeon divides the proximal mesentery and prepares to extract a small segment transrectally.

The surgeon creates an anastomosis by placing a stitch on the left lateral side and another directly posterior. The posterior lip is inverted with careful stitching, ensuring good inward opposition as the suturing progresses around to the left.
The surgeon begins suturing the second layer of the anastomosis, moving around to the right side with a baseball stitch on the posterior row. Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied for visualization.

The surgeon converts the anastomosis to a canal over the top, addressing a size discrepancy between the rectum and proximal bowel by taking larger, full-thickness bites on the rectal side. The anterior lip is inverted to prevent bulging mucosa, with a posterior trio silk suture maintaining inversion on the posterior row despite the baseball stitch technique.

The surgeon ties two sutures together for added security at the anastomosis site, ensuring the tissue folds inward nicely. A leak test and rigid proctoscopy are performed through the anastomosis, confirming it is widely patent with no bleeding or leaks.
